subject: [PATCH v3 0/6] mm: access remote process memory under the per-VMA lock

reviews.author: Usama Arif
reviews.summary: Reviewer requested a formal Acked-by tag in the next patch revision, providing their explicit approval for the changes.
reviews.raw_body:
Hi Rik,

Would you mind changing this to

Acked-by: Usama Arif <usama.arif@linux.dev>

in the next revision

Thanks!

reviews.author: Usama Arif
reviews.summary:
The reviewer pointed out that the current behavior of __access_remote_vm() is not yet changed to use the per-VMA lock, as described in a later patch.

Reviewer Usama Arif expressed concern that the patch may not be stable due to repeated changes in tagged address control, potentially triggered by CLONE_VM operations.

Reviewer noted that the per-VMA lock is not sufficient to protect against concurrent access from other VMA's, as it does not prevent page table updates while holding the lock.
reviews.raw_body:
This describes behavior introduced in a later patch, not by this patch.
At this point, __access_remote_vm() still acquires mmap_lock before
untagging the address.

---

I think it might not be stable? set_tagged_addr_ctrl() can change it
repeatedly until a CLONE_VM operation sets MM_CONTEXT_LOCK_PMLEN.

---

_______________________________________________
linux-riscv mailing list
linux-riscv@lists.infradead.org
http://lists.infradead.org/mailman/listinfo/linux-riscv

reviews.author: Usama Arif
reviews.summary:
Reviewer noted that faultin_page() does not acquire the mmap lock when called from __access_remote_vm() with FOLL_VMA_LOCK, potentially causing a lock ordering issue.

Reviewer Usama Arif noted that check_vma_flags() returns early for VM_IO and VM_PFNMAP, allowing non-COWed pages to be returned from a PROT_NONE PFNMAP VMA, and requested the function's logic be restructured.

Reviewer Usama Arif noted that __get_user_pages() calls flush_anon_page() and flush_dcache_page() before returning a page, and asked if the same should be done in the current code path.
reviews.raw_body:
Does the above line also need to be changed? mmap_lock is not held on entry in
faultin_page() if passing FOLL_VMA_LOCK, right?

---

Do you need to somehow restructure check_vma_flags()?

The first thing that check_vma_flags() does is:

	if (vm_flags & (VM_IO | VM_PFNMAP))
		return -EFAULT;

check_vma_flags() returns before evaluating VM_READ,
FOLL_FORCE, FOLL_ANON or the architecture permission check.

The code then ignores that early error and may return a COWed
page through follow_page_mask(). A non-FOLL_FORCE caller
can therefore read a COWed page from a PROT_NONE PFNMAP VMA.

---

__get_user_pages() does

	flush_anon_page(vma, subpage, start + j * PAGE_SIZE);
	flush_dcache_page(subpage);

before returning the page. Do you need to that here as well above?

reviews.author: Rik Riel (author)
reviews.summary: Author acknowledged a concern about ARM tagged addresses and their implications for VMAs, agreed to clean up the changelog, but did not address whether the patch will need further revision to handle this issue.
sentiment_signals: agreed to clean up the changelog
reviews.raw_body:
You're right, ARM tagged addresses seem to work a
little differently from x86 LAM.

It looks like on ARM with MTE, the top 8 bits of
the virtual address are available as tags, meaning
that address space cannot be used for VMAs.

For purposes of accessing process memory, this
is a little more stable than LAM, because the
number of bits we need to mask out of the address
is always the same when MTE is in use.

At least, I think so. Am I overlooking anything?

I will clean up the changelog like you suggested.

-- 
All Rights Reversed.

_______________________________________________
linux-riscv mailing list
linux-riscv@lists.infradead.org
http://lists.infradead.org/mailman/listinfo/linux-riscv

reviews.author: Rik Riel (author)
reviews.summary:
Author acknowledged that the current behavior of falling back to ->access() hook is correct, even though the implementation needs improvement. They proposed a change to check_vma_flags() to ignore specified flags and specifically suggested ignoring VM_IO | VM_PFNMAP in this code path.

Author acknowledged a concern about virtually indexed caches and proposed restructuring get_user_page_vma_remote() to improve performance by avoiding double VMA lookups.
reviews.raw_body:
In this code path, if we return an error to
__access_remote_vm(), we end up falling back
to the ->access() hook, and will end up reading
the memory that's "under" that COW, instead of
the COWed page that is currently in the process
we are accessing.

In other words, I think the behavior of this
code is the way we want, even though the
implementation should be cleaned up.

How about we change check_vma_flags() so it
ignores specified flags?

check_vma_flags(vma, gup_flags, ignore_flags)

Then this one call to check_vma_flags can have
it ignore VM_IO | VM_PFNMAP, since we want to
access the COWed pages in those VMAs in this
code path, but fall back to ->access when
there are no COW pages.

---

I suppose on systems with virtually indexed caches
we do need that.

Thanks for spotting that!

Now I also wonder if I could restructure get_user_page_vma_remote()
to do the lookup before the get_user_pages_remote(), and have
it then use the new get_user_page_vma() function, to avoid
the double VMA lookup (why are we doing that, anyway?)

reviews.author: Usama Arif
reviews.summary: The reviewer noted that the patch does not handle the case where a page is already mapped in another process, and requested that the patch be updated to properly handle this scenario.

reviews.author: Rik Riel (author)
reviews.summary: Author acknowledged that RISCV is similar to x86 in terms of pointer masking, but questioned the assumption that a process would have all its VMAs in the masked-off area when accessing a remote process's memory.
reviews.raw_body:
Ugh, I looked at the wrong one.  RISCV is like
x86, with a dynamic (though a limited number
of options?) mask.

Having said that, won't a process have all of
its VMAs in the masked-off area, so coming in
from a remote process to a valid memory address
should already give us a valid address?

-- 
All Rights Reversed.

_______________________________________________
linux-riscv mailing list
linux-riscv@lists.infradead.org
http://lists.infradead.org/mailman/listinfo/linux-riscv

reviews.author: Usama Arif
reviews.summary: The reviewer noted that the new order of operations in __access_remote_vm() can lead to a concurrent PMLEN change between reading pmlen and attempting the per-VMA lookup, potentially causing issues due to the lack of serialization between these two operations.
reviews.raw_body:
So my understanding from exploring this code is, and hopefully someone
in CC from riscv can correct me, for example:

Tagged pointer:  0xabcd000012345678
PMLEN 16:        0x0000000012345678
PMLEN 7:         0xffcd000012345678

The target VMA might be at 0x12345678, but applying PMLEN 7
to that tagged pointer does not produce that address.

Previously, the order was:

Take mmap read lock.
Read pmlen.
Untag the address.
Look up the VMA.

Changing PMLEN takes the mmap write lock. The read and write
operations were therefore serialized.

The new order is:

Read pmlen without mmap lock.
Untag the address.
Attempt the per-VMA lookup.
Possibly take mmap lock later.
Continue using the already-untagged address.

A concurrent PMLEN change can occur between those operations?



_______________________________________________
linux-riscv mailing list
linux-riscv@lists.infradead.org
http://lists.infradead.org/mailman/listinfo/linux-riscv

reviews.author: Rik Riel (author)
reviews.summary: The author is addressing concerns about the behavior of __access_remote_vm() when dealing with tagged addresses and VMA lookups. They are asking for clarification on possible outcomes, specifically what happens if the address is already untagged or if tagging is disabled while waiting for the mmap_lock.
reviews.raw_body:
I suppose it could, but what are the possible outcomes here?

- We fail to untag the address, the vma lookup
  fails, and we fail to access memory.

- The address is already untagged, maps to a
  VMA, and the access succeeds.

Are there any others?

The VMAs of the process need to be in the bottom
part of the address space, right? The part where
untagged addresses sit.

For things like /proc/<pid>/cmdline we should
automatically get an address without any of the
high bits set.

For things like ptrace peek / poke, BPF process
accesses, and others, I really do not know if
those could get tagged addresses...

What are the failures we need to protect against?

What if something comes in with a tagged address,
but the process disables tagging while that
something waits for the mmap_lock?

Does that reproduce the failure case, without
any locking changes?

reviews.author: Usama Arif
reviews.summary:
Reviewer Usama Arif raised a concern about tagged pointers becoming invalid due to changes in PMLEN before an mmap-locked lookup, and suggested seeking input from RISC-V developers for this issue.

Reviewer noted that the per-VMA lock is not sufficient for RISC-V, as it does not handle the case where a page's physical address crosses a VMA boundary, and suggested adding additional checks to handle this scenario.
reviews.raw_body:
So I think the above question is what needs to be
answered.
A tagged pointer can become invalid if PMLEN changes
before the old mmap-locked lookup too.
The mmap lock only defined whether the lookup observed
the old or new mode.

For VMA as you said, it should be ok. I don't know about
others. I think it would be best to get input from
RISC-V folks for this. Hopefully its ok..
If it is ok, then all that would be need to be done
is to just remove in the commit message that pmlen
is stable.

---

_______________________________________________
linux-riscv mailing list
linux-riscv@lists.infradead.org
http://lists.infradead.org/mailman/listinfo/linux-riscv

2026-07-20.patch_summary: This patch series improves the way Linux accesses remote process memory by introducing a per-VMA lock in __access_remote_vm(). The per-VMA lock is used when accessing memory within a single VMA, and falls back to the mmap lock for cross-VMA accesses or when other conditions are met. A new gup helper, get_user_page_vma(), is also introduced to fault in missing pages under either lock. This change aims to reduce contention on the mmap lock and improve system reliability by preventing system monitoring software from getting stuck behind a locked process.

reviews.author: David (Arm)
reviews.summary:
The reviewer notes that the GUP code previously rejected VM_IO/VM_PFNMAP due to incorrect handling of vm_normal_page_pmd(), but this issue is not addressed in the current patch series.

Reviewer David (Arm) expressed initial positive sentiment towards the patch series, but stated that it will take him time to thoroughly review the details.
sentiment_signals: patch does not address a known issue
reviews.raw_body:
Yep. The GUP code rejected VM_IO/VM_PFNMAP for, though, because it did not
properly handle vm_normal_page_pmd() etc so far.

---

It will take me a bit to dig into the details; I'll be out the remainder of the
week. But the general direction sounds good.

-- 
Cheers,

David

2026-07-21.patch_summary: This patch series improves the way Linux accesses remote process memory by introducing a per-VMA lock in __access_remote_vm(). The per-VMA lock is used when accessing memory within a single VMA, and falls back to the mmap lock for cross-VMA accesses or when other conditions are met. A new gup helper, get_user_page_vma(), is also introduced to fault in missing pages under either lock. This change aims to reduce contention on the mmap lock and improve system reliability by preventing system monitoring software from getting stuck behind a locked process.

reviews.author: Rik Riel (author)
reviews.summary:
Author acknowledged that the patch series is getting too large and promised to limit future additions.

The author is inquiring about whether a PMD-sized COW on VM_IO/VM_PFNMAP VMA should be addressed immediately or left for later, indicating that the patch can proceed without this specific fix.
reviews.raw_body:
I'll stop making this series (much) bigger, then ;)

I added a few things for v4, but I can save other
stuff for follow-ups.

---

Do we ever do a PMD sized COW on VM_IO / VM_PFNMAP
VMAs?

In other words, is this something we should:
- leave out for now?
- fix now?
- fix later?

If we can fix it later, I'll add it to the list.

-- 
All Rights Reversed.

2026-07-22.patch_summary: This patch series improves the way Linux accesses remote process memory by introducing a per-VMA lock in __access_remote_vm(). The per-VMA lock is used when accessing memory within a single VMA, and falls back to the mmap lock for cross-VMA accesses or when other conditions are met. A new gup helper, get_user_page_vma(), is also introduced to fault in missing pages under either lock. This change aims to reduce contention on the mmap lock and improve system reliability by preventing system monitoring software from getting stuck behind a locked process.
